sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Acute Decompensated Heart Failure (ADHF) is the sudden or gradual worsening of signs and symptoms of heart failure, resulting in acute respiratory distress and/or systemic congestion. It often occurs in patients with pre-existing chronic heart failure but can also be the initial presentation of cardiac dysfunction from a myocardial infarction (MI) for example. ADHF can be a result of right (peripheral), left (pulmonary) or biventricular failure.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Right Ventricular Failure Left Ventricular Failure Additional Signs & Symptoms
- Peripheral Oedema
- Hypotension
- Jugular vein distention
- Hepatomegaly (enlarged liver)
- Ascites (in advanced cases)
- Acute Dyspnoea (SOB)
- Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
- Orthopnoea (worse lying flat)
- Tachypnoea
- Crepes on auscultation
- Hypoxia or low o2 saturation
- Pink frothy sputum
- Chest discomfort (MI, ischemia possible)
- Tachycardia
- Cool, clammy skin
- Cyanosis
- Altered mental status
- Cardiac Arrhythmia
- Lethargy

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Right sided failure or preload-dependent states: avoid nitrates unless the diagnosis and haemodynamics clearly support their use
- Oxygen should be given only if indicated by hypoxaemia or significant respiratory distress; patient distress alone is not a reason to force oxygen if saturation is adequate
- Mental confusion or altered level of consciousness may occur due to hypoxia
- Coughing is a frequent complaint that is suggestive of LVF
- Do not allow the patient to walk
- Use clinical judgment to differentiate from sepsis, pneumonia, asthma/COPD, pulmonary embolus, or other causes of dyspnoea
- Causes to consider include infarction, valvular regurgitation, myocarditis, cardiomyopathy, medication toxicity, PE, dysrhythmias, anaemia, drowning, high altitude or decompression illnesses, burns, pancreatitis, or pregnancy

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Open, clear and maintain the airway
- Administer oxygen and ventilatory support if required; titrate oxygen to clinical need rather than using it routinely in non-hypoxaemic patients
- If blood pressure permits, position sitting or semi-recumbent in a position of comfort
- Vital Signs
- Limb lead ECG

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 lead ECG
- Establish vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy only if hypovolaemia is clearly indicated, using cautious aliquots
- Consider Aspirin if ACS is suspected and there is no clear contraindication
- Consider GTN in hypertensive acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema if blood pressure is adequate and there is no suspected preload-dependent state, in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider loop diuretic therapy in consultation with on-call doctor when fluid overload is evident
- Manage the underlying condition if known as per the relevant guideline
